lookAlikes.name: Yellow Stainer (Agaricus xanthodermus)
lookAlikes.distinction: The critical look-alike to learn.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous possible confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Field Mushroom (Agaricus campestris)
lookAlikes.distinction: The classic 'button mushroom' of fields and pastures.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: Young white buttons of A.

lookAlikes.name: Field Mushroom (Agaricus campestris)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very closely related and nearly identical in the field.

lookAlikes.name: Yellow-staining Mushroom (Agaricus xanthodermus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Looks similar in the button stage.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: The critical confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Yellow-staining Mushroom (Agaricus xanthodermus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Looks very similar — same white cap, same habitat.

lookAlikes.name: White Dapperling (Leucoagaricus leucothites)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows in similar grassy habitats and looks like a white Field Mushroom at first glance.

lookAlikes.name: Horse Mushroom (Agaricus arvensis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also stains slightly yellowish, but the color is a dull ochre-yellow, not the intense chrome-yellow of the Yellow Stainer.

lookAlikes.name: Field Mushroom (Agaricus campestris)
lookAlikes.distinction: No yellow staining whatsoever — flesh stays white when cut or scratched.

lookAlikes.name: Button Mushroom (Agaricus bisporus)
lookAlikes.distinction: The cultivated supermarket mushroom.

lookAlikes.name: Conocybe filaris (Deadly Conocybe)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY.

lookAlikes.name: Agrocybe pediades (Common Fieldcap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar, also edible.

lookAlikes.name: Inocybe species (Fibercaps)
lookAlikes.distinction: Many Inocybe species are toxic, containing muscarine.

lookAlikes.name: Fly Agaric (Amanita muscaria)
lookAlikes.distinction: This is the critical confusion.

lookAlikes.name: American Caesar's (Amanita jacksonii)
lookAlikes.distinction: The North American counterpart.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: The egg stage of Caesar's Mushroom can be confused with the egg stage of the Death Cap — both emerge from a white universal veil.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: The critical confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Destroying Angel (Amanita virosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Pure white all over, with a shaggy stem, sack-like volva, and no potato smell.

lookAlikes.name: The Blusher (Amanita rubescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Pinkish-brown cap with grayish veil patches.

lookAlikes.name: Caesar's Mushroom (Amanita caesarea)
lookAlikes.distinction: The prized edible.

lookAlikes.name: Fly Agaric (Amanita muscaria)
lookAlikes.distinction: The classic red-and-white toadstool.

lookAlikes.name: Panther Cap (Amanita pantherina)
lookAlikes.distinction: Brown to tan cap rather than yellow.

lookAlikes.name: Amanita caesarea (Caesar's Mushroom)
lookAlikes.distinction: The European counterpart.

lookAlikes.name: Amanita muscaria (Fly Agaric)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also has a red cap but is covered in white wart-like patches (remnants of universal veil).

lookAlikes.name: Amanita phalloides (Death Cap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Greenish to yellowish cap (not red).

lookAlikes.name: Caesar's Mushroom (Amanita caesarea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Actually edible and delicious — prized since Roman times.

lookAlikes.name: Panther Cap (Amanita pantherina)
lookAlikes.distinction: More dangerous than the Fly Agaric.

lookAlikes.name: Yellow Patches (Amanita flavoconia)
lookAlikes.distinction: Eastern North American species.

lookAlikes.name: The Blusher (Amanita rubescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Grey Spotted Amanita (Amanita excelsa var. spissa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar brown cap with grayish warts, but the warts are gray rather than pure white, the volva is less distinctly rimmed, and the flesh does not cause toxic reactions.

lookAlikes.name: Fly Agaric (Amanita muscaria)
lookAlikes.distinction: Classic red cap with white warts is usually obvious, but faded or rain-washed Fly Agaric specimens can appear orange-brown and overlap with Panther Cap.

lookAlikes.name: Paddy Straw Mushroom (Volvariella volvacea)
lookAlikes.distinction: A popular edible mushroom in Southeast Asian cuisine.

lookAlikes.name: Green-spored Parasol (Chlorophyllum molybdites)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most commonly eaten poisonous mushroom in North America (causes severe GI distress but is rarely fatal).

lookAlikes.name: Field Mushroom (Agaricus campestris)
lookAlikes.distinction: A common edible mushroom.

lookAlikes.name: Panther Cap (Amanita pantherina)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous confusion species.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: The world's deadliest mushroom.

lookAlikes.name: Jewelled Amanita (Amanita gemmata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Cap is bright yellow to pale yellow-orange, covered with white warts.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: THE critical look-alike.

lookAlikes.name: Orange Grisette (Amanita crocea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar to A.

lookAlikes.name: Grisette (Amanita vaginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: The gray version of the grisette group.

lookAlikes.name: Field Mushroom (Agaricus campestris)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Eastern North American Destroying Angel (Amanita bisporigera)
lookAlikes.distinction: Essentially the American counterpart of Amanita virosa — equally deadly.

lookAlikes.name: White Dapperling (Leucocoprinus leucothites)
lookAlikes.distinction: A white mushroom that grows in grass and gardens, sometimes confused with both Field Mushrooms and Destroying Angels.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Galerina (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: This is the dangerous one.

lookAlikes.name: Shaggy Scalycap (Pholiota squarrosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows in similar clusters on wood and can overlap in season and habitat.

lookAlikes.name: Ringless Honey Mushroom (Desarmillaria tabescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar to the honey fungus and also edible when cooked.

lookAlikes.name: Exidia glandulosa (Black Witches' Butter)
lookAlikes.distinction: A dark, gelatinous fungus that also grows on dead hardwood branches.

lookAlikes.name: Tremella mesenterica (Yellow Brain Fungus)
lookAlikes.distinction: A bright yellow to orange gelatinous fungus on dead hardwood.

lookAlikes.name: Bitter Bolete (Tylopilus felleus)
lookAlikes.distinction: The classic porcini impostor.

lookAlikes.name: White King Bolete (Boletus barrowsii)
lookAlikes.distinction: Actually edible and delicious — just paler.

lookAlikes.name: Bay Bolete (Imleria badia)
lookAlikes.distinction: Another edible look-alike, common in European pine forests.

lookAlikes.name: Boletus edulis (Porcini/Cep)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very closely related and culinarily equivalent.

lookAlikes.name: Tylopilus felleus (Bitter Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Can superficially resemble a porcini-type bolete with a brown cap and reticulated stem.

lookAlikes.name: Rubroboletus satanas (Satan's Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: A toxic bolete with a pale grayish-white cap (not dark reddish-brown), blood-red pore surface, and red reticulation on the stem.

lookAlikes.name: Boletus edulis (King Bolete / Porcini)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar and equally edible.

lookAlikes.name: Tylopilus felleus (Bitter Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar shape and brown cap but has pink to pinkish-brown pores (not white to yellow-green) and a brown reticulation on the stem (not white).

lookAlikes.name: Rubroboletus satanas (Satan's Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Pale whitish cap but with vivid red pores and a red-orange reticulation on a swollen yellow and red stem.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Fibrecap (Inocybe erubescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: TOXIC.

lookAlikes.name: Sweetbread Mushroom (Clitopilus prunulus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also white with a strong mealy smell, making confusion possible.

lookAlikes.name: Common Earthball (Scleroderma citrinum)
lookAlikes.distinction: The dangerous one to watch for.

lookAlikes.name: Common Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much smaller — golf ball to tennis ball sized.

lookAlikes.name: Purple-Spored Puffball (Calvatia cyathiformis)
lookAlikes.distinction: A close relative that's also edible when young.

lookAlikes.name: Jack O'Lantern (Omphalotus olearius)
lookAlikes.distinction: The dangerous one.

lookAlikes.name: False Chanterelle (Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more common mix-up than Jack O'Lantern.

lookAlikes.name: Scaly Vase Chanterelle (Turbinellus floccosus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Vase-shaped like a chanterelle but covered in coarse, shaggy scales on the cap surface.

lookAlikes.name: Jack O'Lantern (Omphalotus olearius)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous chanterelle look-alike.

lookAlikes.name: False Chanterelle (Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca)
lookAlikes.distinction: Thinner-fleshed and more orange than golden.

lookAlikes.name: Golden Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius)
lookAlikes.distinction: The European counterpart — extremely similar and historically confused with C.

lookAlikes.name: Cantharellus cibarius (Golden Chanterelle)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger (up to 12 cm), golden yellow rather than red-pink.

lookAlikes.name: Hygrocybe miniata (Vermilion Waxcap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar red color but has true blade-like gills, not false ridges.

lookAlikes.name: Craterellus tubaeformis (Trumpet Chanterelle)
lookAlikes.distinction: Taxonomists have moved some populations into Craterellus.

lookAlikes.name: Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca (False Chanterelle)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has true, thin, blade-like gills rather than blunt ridges.

lookAlikes.name: Omphalotus olearius (Jack O'Lantern)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger, deeper orange, and grows in dense clusters on wood or buried roots.

lookAlikes.name: Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Bright orange-yellow shelves with no scales — impossible to confuse once you know both.

lookAlikes.name: Hen of the Woods (Grifola frondosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows as a large rosette of many small, overlapping gray-brown caps at the base of oaks.

lookAlikes.name: Birch Polypore (Fomitopsis betulina)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smooth, white to gray-brown brackets exclusively on birch trees.

lookAlikes.name: Chlorociboria aeruginosa
lookAlikes.distinction: An extremely similar sister species that can only be reliably distinguished by microscopic spore measurements.

lookAlikes.name: Ophiostoma (Blue Stain Fungi)
lookAlikes.distinction: Various blue-stain fungi can discolor wood blue-gray, but the color is duller and grayish rather than the vivid turquoise of Chlorociboria.

lookAlikes.name: Parasol Mushroom (Macrolepiota procera)
lookAlikes.distinction: Excellent edible.

lookAlikes.name: Shaggy Parasol (Chlorophyllum rhacodes)
lookAlikes.distinction: Edible with caution (causes GI upset in some).

lookAlikes.name: Agaricus campestris (Meadow Mushroom)
lookAlikes.distinction: Edible.

lookAlikes.name: Green-spored Parasol (Chlorophyllum molybdites)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous look-alike and the #1 cause of mushroom poisonings in North America.

lookAlikes.name: Parasol Mushroom (Macrolepiota procera)
lookAlikes.distinction: A prized edible that's taller and more slender.

lookAlikes.name: Yellow-staining Mushroom (Agaricus xanthodermus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Toxic Agaricus species that causes GI upset.

lookAlikes.name: Clathrus archeri (Devil's Fingers)
lookAlikes.distinction: Instead of a lattice cage, devil's fingers produce 4-8 separate tentacle-like arms that spread outward from a central base.

lookAlikes.name: Colus hirudinosus (Stinky Squid)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar lattice structure but with fewer, thicker arms that are fused at the top, forming a column rather than a sphere.

lookAlikes.name: Livid Pinkgill (Entoloma sinuatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: A dangerous look-alike.

lookAlikes.name: Ivory Funnel (Clitocybe dealbata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much smaller (cap 2–6 cm vs.

lookAlikes.name: Wood Blewit (Lepista nuda)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar size and woodland habitat, and also grows in leaf litter.

lookAlikes.name: Fairy Ring Champignon (Marasmius oreades)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Sweetbread Mushroom (Clitopilus prunulus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar size and white color, but Clitopilus prunulus has pink-tinged gills at maturity, a pink spore print, and a strong mealy smell.

lookAlikes.name: Fool's Funnel (Clitocybe rivulosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Extremely similar and possibly the same species; some mycologists treat them as synonyms.

lookAlikes.name: Cortinarius species (violet Corts)
lookAlikes.distinction: Several Cortinarius species have violet to purple coloring and can superficially resemble Blewits.

lookAlikes.name: Sordid Blewit (Collybia sordida)
lookAlikes.distinction: A smaller, thinner-fleshed species with similar violet coloring.

lookAlikes.name: Entoloma sinuatum (Livid Pinkgill)
lookAlikes.distinction: A toxic species that can have a faintly lilac-tinged cap, though it is typically more gray-buff than truly violet.

lookAlikes.name: Psilocybe cyanescens (Wavy Cap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also small and brown, also found on wood chips.

lookAlikes.name: Agrocybe praecox (Spring Fieldcap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Larger (3-8 cm cap), sturdier build, with a more persistent ring and a pale buff to yellowish cap.

lookAlikes.name: Galerina marginata (Funeral Bell)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also deadly, also has amatoxins and a rusty brown spore print.

lookAlikes.name: Shaggy Ink Cap (Coprinus comatus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Taller and more cylindrical with shaggy, upturned white scales covering the cap — looks like a lawyer's wig.

lookAlikes.name: Mica Cap (Coprinellus micaceus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller and more delicate with a tawny-brown cap covered in tiny glistening granules (like mica flakes) when young.

lookAlikes.name: Hare's Foot Ink Cap (Coprinopsis lagopus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more fragile and covered in dense white woolly fibers when young, giving it a fuzzy appearance.

lookAlikes.name: Common Ink Cap (Coprinopsis atramentaria)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also deliquesces into ink, but the cap is smooth and gray-brown — no shaggy scales.

lookAlikes.name: Green-Spored Parasol (Chlorophyllum molybdites)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most commonly eaten poisonous mushroom in North America.

lookAlikes.name: Magpie Fungus (Coprinopsis picacea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has a similar tall, elongated shape with patches on the cap, but the patches are white on a dark brown-black background (the inverse of Shaggy Mane's pattern).

lookAlikes.name: Coprinopsis atramentaria (Common Inkcap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more common, lacks the white patches.

lookAlikes.name: Coprinus comatus (Shaggy Mane)
lookAlikes.distinction: Edible (before deliquescence).

lookAlikes.name: Coprinellus domesticus (Firerug Inkcap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller, orange-brown cap, grows on decaying wood indoors and outdoors.

lookAlikes.name: Tolypocladium capitatum
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in appearance but typically associated with moth pupae in North American coniferous forests.

lookAlikes.name: Orange Club Fungi (Clavulinopsis species)
lookAlikes.distinction: Some small orange club fungi could be confused at a glance.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Webcap (Cortinarius rubellus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Equally deadly cousin.

lookAlikes.name: Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar warm orange-brown color, but chanterelles have thick forking ridges instead of true gills, a funnel shape, and a fruity apricot aroma.

lookAlikes.name: Cortinarius cinnamomeus (Cinnamon Webcap)
lookAlikes.distinction: A smaller, more yellowish-brown Cortinarius that is not considered dangerously toxic.

lookAlikes.name: Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius)
lookAlikes.distinction: The confusion that nearly killed Nicholas Evans.

lookAlikes.name: Fool's Webcap (Cortinarius orellanus)
lookAlikes.distinction: An equally deadly relative.

lookAlikes.name: Honey Fungus (Armillaria mellea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows in clusters on wood (not from soil), has a white spore print, and a prominent ring on the stem.

lookAlikes.name: Yellowfoot Chanterelle (Craterellus tubaeformis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar trumpet shape but yellowish-brown cap and distinctive yellow-orange stem.

lookAlikes.name: Devil's Urn (Urnula craterium)
lookAlikes.distinction: Cup-shaped rather than trumpet-shaped, with a leathery, dark brown to black exterior.

lookAlikes.name: Sinuous Chanterelle (Craterellus cinereus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar dark gray-brown color but has well-defined gill-like ridges on the outer surface rather than a smooth or slightly wrinkled exterior.

lookAlikes.name: Cantharellus tubaeformis (Yellowfoot Chanterelle)
lookAlikes.distinction: Essentially the same species under the old classification.

lookAlikes.name: Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca (False Chanterelle)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has true, thin, blade-like gills rather than blunt ridges.

lookAlikes.name: Funeral Bell (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY.

lookAlikes.name: Sheathed Woodtuft (Kuehneromyces mutabilis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Edible but could cause confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Beech Woodwart (Hypoxylon fragiforme)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much smaller (typically under 1 cm across), brick-red to reddish-brown rather than black, and found specifically on dead beech bark.

lookAlikes.name: Cramp Ball Lookalike (Annulohypoxylon thouarsianum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar dark, rounded fruiting bodies found primarily on oak rather than ash.

lookAlikes.name: Black Bulgar (Bulgaria inquinans)
lookAlikes.distinction: Superficially similar dark, rounded growths on dead wood, but Bulgaria inquinans is soft and gelatinous — rubbery to the touch, not rock-hard like Daldinia.

lookAlikes.name: St. George's Mushroom (Calocybe gambosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Fruits in spring (April-May), not autumn.

lookAlikes.name: Field Mushroom (Agaricus campestris)
lookAlikes.distinction: Field Mushrooms have chocolate-brown to dark brown spore prints and gills that start pink and darken to brown-black.

lookAlikes.name: Clouded Funnel (Clitocybe nebularis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar size and grayish-white color, but the Clouded Funnel has decurrent gills (running down the stem), a white spore print, and a distinctly funnel-shaped mature cap.

lookAlikes.name: Liver-colored Polypore (Ganoderma species)
lookAlikes.distinction: Some Ganoderma brackets can appear reddish-brown, but they are woody, hard, and perennial, not soft and fleshy.

lookAlikes.name: Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also a bracket fungus on oaks, but bright orange-yellow rather than blood-red.

lookAlikes.name: Galerina marginata (Deadly Galerina)
lookAlikes.distinction: A critically dangerous look-alike that also grows on wood and has a similar tawny-brown cap.

lookAlikes.name: Xeromphalina campanella
lookAlikes.distinction: A small orange-brown mushroom that grows in dense clusters on conifer wood.

lookAlikes.name: Dryad's Saddle (Cerioporus squamosus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has prominent dark brown scales on the upper surface in a concentric pattern — Birch Polypore is completely smooth.

lookAlikes.name: Artist's Bracket (Ganoderma applanatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much harder and woodier, with a rough, concentrically zoned upper surface that is gray-brown to dark brown.

lookAlikes.name: Piptoporus (synonym)
lookAlikes.distinction: Not actually a different species — Piptoporus betulinus is the old name for Fomitopsis betulina.

lookAlikes.name: Sheathed Woodtuft (Kuehneromyces mutabilis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Popular edible in Europe.

lookAlikes.name: Honey Mushroom (Armillaria mellea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Edible when cooked.

lookAlikes.name: Wavy Cap (Psilocybe cyanescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Psychoactive, grows on wood chips.

lookAlikes.name: Artist's Conk (Ganoderma applanatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Flatter, harder, and lacks the glossy lacquered surface of Reishi.

lookAlikes.name: Hemlock Varnish Shelf (Ganoderma tsugae)
lookAlikes.distinction: Extremely similar to Reishi — glossy, reddish, same shape.

lookAlikes.name: Sessile Reishi (Ganoderma sessile)
lookAlikes.distinction: The North American species most often confused with G.

lookAlikes.name: Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus sulphureus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Bright orange and yellow shelves — impossible to miss the color difference.

lookAlikes.name: Berkeley's Polypore (Bondarzewia berkeleyi)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger individual caps (up to 25 cm each) with a pale cream to tan color.

lookAlikes.name: Black-staining Polypore (Meripilus sumstinei)
lookAlikes.distinction: The giveaway is in the name — the pore surface and flesh stain black when bruised or cut.

lookAlikes.name: Honey Mushroom (Armillaria mellea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Edible when cooked.

lookAlikes.name: Jack O'Lantern (Omphalotus olearius)
lookAlikes.distinction: Toxic.

lookAlikes.name: Gymnopilus luteofolius (Yellow-gilled Gymnopilus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also in the genus Gymnopilus and also found on wood.

lookAlikes.name: True Morel (Morchella esculenta)
lookAlikes.distinction: This is THE critical confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Wrinkled Thimble-Cap (Verpa bohemica)
lookAlikes.distinction: Verpa bohemica has a cap that hangs freely from the stem like a thimble — attached only at the very top.

lookAlikes.name: Snow Morel (Discina gigas)
lookAlikes.distinction: A close relative that's generally larger and lighter in color — tan to yellowish-brown rather than dark reddish-brown.

lookAlikes.name: Lion's Mane (Hericium erinaceus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Forms a single, unbranched, globose clump of long cascading spines (often 2–5 cm long) rather than a branching coral-like structure.

lookAlikes.name: Bear's Head Tooth (Hericium americanum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Intermediate between Coral Tooth and Lion's Mane — it branches, but less extensively than H.

lookAlikes.name: Ochre Spreading Tooth (Steccherinum ochraceum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Completely different in size and habit.

lookAlikes.name: Bear's Head Tooth (Hericium americanum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar but has branching structure — spines hang from multiple short branches rather than a single compact mass.

lookAlikes.name: Coral Tooth (Hericium coralloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more branched and coral-like.

lookAlikes.name: Tiered Tooth (Hericium cirrhatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows in shelf-like or tiered layers rather than a single globular mass.

lookAlikes.name: Depressed Hedgehog (Hydnum umbilicatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller and darker orange than H.

lookAlikes.name: Shingled Hedgehog (Sarcodon imbricatus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger and darker, with coarse brown scales on the cap arranged like roof shingles.

lookAlikes.name: Golden Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar color but has ridges (false gills) running down the stem — NOT spines.

lookAlikes.name: Crimson Waxcap (Hygrocybe punicea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Larger (cap up to 12 cm), with a more conical cap shape and a fibrous, streaky stem.

lookAlikes.name: Vermilion Waxcap (Hygrocybe miniata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller (cap usually under 3 cm), with a more finely scaly cap surface and a thinner stem.

lookAlikes.name: Golden Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius)
lookAlikes.distinction: The prized original that the False Chanterelle imitates.

lookAlikes.name: Jack O'Lantern (Omphalotus olearius)
lookAlikes.distinction: TOXIC — causes severe vomiting and diarrhea.

lookAlikes.name: Pacific Chanterelle (Cantharellus formosus)
lookAlikes.distinction: The Pacific Northwest's premier chanterelle.

lookAlikes.name: Sulphur Tuft (Hypholoma fasciculare)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most common confusion species.

lookAlikes.name: Funeral Bell (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY — contains amatoxins that destroy the liver, the same toxins found in the Death Cap.

lookAlikes.name: Honey Fungus (Armillaria mellea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also grows in dense clusters on wood, but Honey Fungus has a prominent white ring on the stem, white spore print (not purplish-brown), and often has small dark scales on the cap center.

lookAlikes.name: Sheathed Woodtuft (Kuehneromyces mutabilis)
lookAlikes.distinction: An edible species that also grows in clusters on dead wood.

lookAlikes.name: Brick Cap (Hypholoma lateritium)
lookAlikes.distinction: A related species considered edible in some regions.

lookAlikes.name: Honey Fungus (Armillaria mellea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also grows in clusters on wood, but has a white spore print, a prominent ring on the stem, and honey-brown coloring.

lookAlikes.name: Inocybe erubescens (Red-Staining Inocybe)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY POISONOUS — contains high levels of muscarine.

lookAlikes.name: Inocybe geophylla
lookAlikes.distinction: Contains muscarine — poisonous.

lookAlikes.name: Liberty Cap (Psilocybe semilanceata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also psilocybin-containing but very different habitat (grasslands, not under trees).

lookAlikes.name: St. George's Mushroom (Calocybe gambosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous confusion.

lookAlikes.name: White Fibrecap (Inocybe geophylla)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much smaller (cap 1–4 cm), pure white to lilac, silky smooth cap, and does NOT stain red.

lookAlikes.name: Torn Fibrecap (Inocybe rimosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Cap is more distinctly radially cracked (rimose) and straw-yellow to ochre without reddish staining.

lookAlikes.name: Porcini (Boletus edulis)
lookAlikes.distinction: The mushroom everyone hopes they've found.

lookAlikes.name: Bitter Bolete (Tylopilus felleus)
lookAlikes.distinction: The bolete that ruins dinners.

lookAlikes.name: Red-cracking Bolete (Xerocomellus chrysenteron)
lookAlikes.distinction: A common bolete whose cap cracks with age, revealing reddish flesh beneath the surface — bay bolete caps don't crack or show red.

lookAlikes.name: Birch Polypore (Fomitopsis betulina)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also grows on birch but looks completely different: a smooth, whitish to grayish bracket with a distinct kidney-shaped form.

lookAlikes.name: Burnt or Burl Wood
lookAlikes.distinction: The most common 'false Chaga' is simply a tree burl (abnormal woody growth) or a patch of fire-damaged bark.

lookAlikes.name: The Deceiver (Laccaria laccata)
lookAlikes.distinction: The orange-brown cousin.

lookAlikes.name: Wood Blewit (Lepista nuda)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger mushroom (cap 6–15 cm) with lilac-blue colors that can overlap with the amethyst deceiver's palette.

lookAlikes.name: Lilac Fibrecap (Inocybe geophylla var. lilacina)
lookAlikes.distinction: POISONOUS.

lookAlikes.name: Amethyst Deceiver (Laccaria amethystina)
lookAlikes.distinction: The purple version of The Deceiver.

lookAlikes.name: Inocybe species
lookAlikes.distinction: Some small brown Inocybe species can superficially resemble dried-out Deceivers.

lookAlikes.name: Mycena species
lookAlikes.distinction: Small Mycena mushrooms share woodland habitats but are generally smaller, more fragile, with very thin stems that snap easily (unlike the tough, fibrous Laccaria stem).

lookAlikes.name: Lactarius deterrimus (False Saffron Milk Cap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in appearance but associated with spruce (Picea) rather than pine.

lookAlikes.name: Lactarius torminosus (Woolly Milk Cap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has a pinkish-orange cap with a distinctly shaggy, woolly margin (hairy fringes on the cap edge).

lookAlikes.name: Lactarius salmonicolor
lookAlikes.distinction: Associated with fir (Abies) rather than pine.

lookAlikes.name: Lactarius chelidonium
lookAlikes.distinction: Paler blue-gray cap, often with olive or brownish tones — lacks the deep saturated indigo of L.

lookAlikes.name: Charcoal Burner (Russula cyanoxantha)
lookAlikes.distinction: Can have bluish-purple cap colors that superficially resemble L.

lookAlikes.name: Lactarius paradoxus
lookAlikes.distinction: Blue-silver cap similar to an aged L.

lookAlikes.name: Saffron Milk Cap (Lactarius deliciosus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Orange rather than pink, and critically, exudes bright orange (not white) latex that stains green when exposed to air.

lookAlikes.name: Downy Milk Cap (Lactarius pubescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar to L.

lookAlikes.name: Blushing Milk Cap (Lactarius controversus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger (cap up to 25 cm), with a whitish cap that develops pink-red blotches.

lookAlikes.name: White-pored Chicken of the Woods (Laetiporus cincinnatus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very closely related and also edible.

lookAlikes.name: Jack O'Lantern (Omphalotus olearius)
lookAlikes.distinction: Orange and grows on wood, so beginners sometimes confuse them — but Jack O'Lanterns have true gills, a central stem, and a classic mushroom shape.

lookAlikes.name: Velvet-top Fungus (Phaeolus schweinitzii)
lookAlikes.distinction: Another bracket fungus found on conifers.

lookAlikes.name: Brown Birch Bolete (Leccinum scabrum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in structure and habitat but the cap is brown rather than orange.

lookAlikes.name: Leccinum vulpinum (Foxy Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Found under pine rather than birch or aspen.

lookAlikes.name: Harrya chromapes (Chrome-footed Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Found in eastern North America.

lookAlikes.name: Orange Birch Bolete (Leccinum aurantiacum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar structure and habitat, but the cap is distinctly orange to reddish-orange rather than brown.

lookAlikes.name: Mottled Bolete (Leccinum variicolor)
lookAlikes.distinction: Cap is mottled with patches of gray, brown, and whitish tones rather than uniform brown.

lookAlikes.name: Bitter Bolete (Tylopilus felleus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Can look similar at a glance, but grows near conifers rather than birch.

lookAlikes.name: Train Wrecker (Neolentinus lepideus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows on conifer wood (shiitake sticks to hardwoods).

lookAlikes.name: Honey Mushroom (Armillaria mellea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows in dense clusters at the base of living or dead trees.

lookAlikes.name: Funeral Bell (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY — contains the same amatoxins as the Death Cap.

lookAlikes.name: Shaggy Parasol (Chlorophyllum rhacodes)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger (cap 10-20 cm), with coarser shaggy scales and flesh that turns orange-red when cut.

lookAlikes.name: Parasol Mushroom (Macrolepiota procera)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger (cap 15-30 cm), with a tall stem marked by a snakeskin pattern and a movable double ring.

lookAlikes.name: Lepiota cristata (Stinking Dapperling)
lookAlikes.distinction: A small Lepiota with reddish-brown concentric scales and a strong, unpleasant rubber or chemical smell.

lookAlikes.name: Wood Blewit (Lepista nuda)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most common confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Entoloma sinuatum (Livid Pinkgill)
lookAlikes.distinction: A toxic grassland species with a similar buff to grayish cap.

lookAlikes.name: Clitocybe rivulosa (Fool's Funnel)
lookAlikes.distinction: A small, white to buff grassland mushroom that contains muscarine and is genuinely dangerous.

lookAlikes.name: Leucocoprinus cretaceus (White Houseplant Mushroom)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in size, shape, and habitat (also appears in potted plants), but entirely white rather than yellow.

lookAlikes.name: Lepiota cristata (Stinking Dapperling)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more dangerous (potentially deadly).

lookAlikes.name: Bolbitius titubans (Yellow Fieldcap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also bright yellow, but found outdoors on grass, dung, or straw, not in houseplants.

lookAlikes.name: Common Earthball (Scleroderma citrinum)
lookAlikes.distinction: This is the dangerous one.

lookAlikes.name: Pear-shaped Puffball (Apioperdon pyriforme)
lookAlikes.distinction: Actually edible too, so less of a worry.

lookAlikes.name: Giant Puffball (Calvatia gigantea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Hard to confuse once mature — Giant Puffballs can reach soccer-ball size or larger.

lookAlikes.name: Lycoperdon perlatum (Gem-studded Puffball)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also edible, also small.

lookAlikes.name: Immature Amanita (Death Cap or Destroying Angel egg)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY.

lookAlikes.name: Scleroderma citrinum (Common Earthball)
lookAlikes.distinction: Toxic.

lookAlikes.name: Shaggy Parasol (Chlorophyllum rhacodes)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller overall and the flesh stains reddish-orange when cut or bruised — the true Parasol does not change color.

lookAlikes.name: Green-Spored Parasol (Chlorophyllum molybdites)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most commonly eaten poisonous mushroom in North America.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Dapperling (Lepiota brunneoincarnata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much smaller (cap under 7 cm) — this is a critical difference.

lookAlikes.name: Fool's Funnel (Clitocybe rivulosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: TOXIC.

lookAlikes.name: Lawn Mottlegill (Panaeolus foenisecii)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also common in lawns but has a dark brown spore print (not white), darker cap, and closely spaced gills that turn dark with spore maturity.

lookAlikes.name: Collared Parachute (Marasmius rotula)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much smaller with a deeply grooved, parasol-like white cap.

lookAlikes.name: Gyromitra esculenta (False Morel)
lookAlikes.distinction: Brain-like, wrinkled surface rather than a honeycomb pattern of pits and ridges.

lookAlikes.name: Verpa bohemica (Early Morel)
lookAlikes.distinction: Cap hangs from the top of the stem like a thimble, attached only at the apex.

lookAlikes.name: Morchella esculenta (Yellow Morel)
lookAlikes.distinction: Lighter colored, with yellowish to tan pits and paler ridges.

lookAlikes.name: Early Morel (Verpa bohemica)
lookAlikes.distinction: The cap hangs free from the stem like a thimble sitting on a finger — attached only at the very top.

lookAlikes.name: False Morel (Gyromitra esculenta)
lookAlikes.distinction: Potentially deadly.

lookAlikes.name: Black Morel (Morchella elata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Actually a true morel and perfectly edible — just a different species.

lookAlikes.name: Mutinus elegans (Elegant Stinkhorn)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar but slightly larger and more vividly colored, with a brighter reddish-orange stalk.

lookAlikes.name: Phallus impudicus (Common Stinkhorn)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger, with a distinct white, skirted, netted cap (indusium) below the gleba-covered head.

lookAlikes.name: Mycena sanguinolenta (Bleeding Bonnet)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar bleeding reaction, but much smaller (cap under 1 cm) and grows on leaf litter and soil rather than on wood.

lookAlikes.name: Mycena galericulata (Common Bonnet)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar size and habitat on wood.

lookAlikes.name: Mycena inclinata (Clustered Bonnet)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also grows in clusters on wood, similar size.

lookAlikes.name: Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius)
lookAlikes.distinction: This is the mix-up that sends people to the ER every year.

lookAlikes.name: Honey Mushroom (Armillaria mellea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also grows in clusters on wood, but Honey Mushrooms have a prominent white ring on the stem, lighter tan-to-brown caps (not bright orange), and white spore prints.

lookAlikes.name: False Chanterelle (Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller and thinner than the Jack O'Lantern, with a more brownish-orange cap that often has a suede-like texture.

lookAlikes.name: Cordyceps militaris
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more common and widespread, found at low elevations across the Northern Hemisphere.

lookAlikes.name: Ophiocordyceps gracilis
lookAlikes.distinction: Found in the same high-altitude habitat and can infect similar host larvae.

lookAlikes.name: Psilocybe cubensis (Golden Teacher)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also psychoactive, same habitat on dung.

lookAlikes.name: Panaeolus antillarum (White Dung Mushroom)
lookAlikes.distinction: Non-psychoactive.

lookAlikes.name: Panaeolus papilionaceus (Petticoat Mottlegill)
lookAlikes.distinction: Non-psychoactive.

lookAlikes.name: Velvet Rollrim (Tapinella atrotomentosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows on dead conifer wood (not from soil), has a dark brown to black velvety stem base, and is significantly larger and more robust.

lookAlikes.name: Ugly Milkcap (Lactarius turpis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar dark brown funnel shape, but exudes white milk when gills are cut or broken.

lookAlikes.name: Brown Funnel Cap (Infundibulicybe gibba)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much thinner-fleshed and smaller, with a pale tan to buff cap and white gills.

lookAlikes.name: Phallus impudicus (Common Stinkhorn)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in shape and ecology, but has a white volva (not pinkish-purple).

lookAlikes.name: Phallus ravenelii (Ravenel's Stinkhorn)
lookAlikes.distinction: Found in eastern North America.

lookAlikes.name: Clathrus ruber (Lattice Stinkhorn)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also a stinkhorn with foul-smelling gleba, but forms a distinctive red lattice or cage structure rather than a phallic stalk.

lookAlikes.name: Dog Stinkhorn (Mutinus caninus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller (5-12 cm tall), more slender, with a tapered orange-red tip rather than a distinct honeycomb head.

lookAlikes.name: Elegant Stinkhorn (Mutinus elegans)
lookAlikes.distinction: Found primarily in eastern North America.

lookAlikes.name: Stinkhorn Egg vs Amanita Egg
lookAlikes.distinction: Young stinkhorn eggs can superficially resemble the egg stage of deadly Amanita species.

lookAlikes.name: Angel Wings (Pleurocybella porrigens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Thinner, more delicate, pure white, and grows exclusively on conifer wood (not hardwood).

lookAlikes.name: Elm Oyster (Hypsizygus ulmarius)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows on living or dead elm and box elder trees.

lookAlikes.name: Ghost Fungus (Omphalotus nidiformis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Found in Australia and parts of Asia.

lookAlikes.name: Oyster Mushroom (Pleurotus ostreatus)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most common confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Angel Wings (Pleurocybella porrigens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Thinner and more delicate than any Pleurotus, pure white, with a translucent quality.

lookAlikes.name: Crepidotus species
lookAlikes.distinction: Small, fan-shaped, stemless mushrooms that grow on dead wood and can superficially resemble young oyster mushrooms.

lookAlikes.name: Pluteus cervinus (Deer Shield)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most common Pluteus species.

lookAlikes.name: Pluteus cyanopus
lookAlikes.distinction: Another rare psilocybin-containing Pluteus species with stronger blue-green tones at the stem base.

lookAlikes.name: Entoloma species
lookAlikes.distinction: Some Entoloma species have similar blue-gray caps and pink spore prints.

lookAlikes.name: Clavulinopsis coral fungi
lookAlikes.distinction: Various edible or harmless coral fungi can be yellow, orange, or pinkish.

lookAlikes.name: Ramaria botrytis (Cauliflower Coral)
lookAlikes.distinction: A large, much-branched coral fungus with white to pinkish branches and purple-pink tips.

lookAlikes.name: Trichoderma cornu-damae
lookAlikes.distinction: The anamorph (asexual stage) of the same organism.

lookAlikes.name: Psilocybe cyanescens (Wavy Cap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also psychoactive, also on wood in the PNW.

lookAlikes.name: Galerina marginata (Funeral Bell)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY.

lookAlikes.name: Stropharia ambigua (Questionable Stropharia)
lookAlikes.distinction: Non-psychoactive.

lookAlikes.name: Teonanácatl (Psilocybe mexicana)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller (cap 1-3 cm vs 2-6 cm), grows in grassy meadows rather than bare disturbed earth.

lookAlikes.name: Golden Teacher (Psilocybe cubensis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Larger, golden-brown mushroom that grows on dung in lowland tropical pastures, not on bare montane soil.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Galerina (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY POISONOUS.

lookAlikes.name: Liberty Cap (Psilocybe semilanceata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also psychoactive, but much smaller and grows in temperate grasslands, not on dung.

lookAlikes.name: Blue Meanie (Panaeolus cyanescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also psychoactive and also grows on dung.

lookAlikes.name: Funeral Bell (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY.

lookAlikes.name: Galerina marginata (Funeral Bell)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY.

lookAlikes.name: Psilocybe allenii
lookAlikes.distinction: Also psychoactive, also on wood chips in the Pacific Northwest.

lookAlikes.name: Hypholoma fasciculare (Sulphur Tuft)
lookAlikes.distinction: Bitter and mildly toxic.

lookAlikes.name: Golden Teacher (Psilocybe cubensis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger (cap 2-8 cm), grows on dung in tropical lowlands, not high-altitude grasslands.

lookAlikes.name: Liberty Cap (Psilocybe semilanceata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar small size and conical cap, but grows in cool temperate grasslands of Europe and North America, not tropical highlands.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Galerina (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY POISONOUS.

lookAlikes.name: Protostropharia semiglobata (Dung Roundhead)
lookAlikes.distinction: Grows directly on dung, not in grass.

lookAlikes.name: Panaeolus semiovatus (Egghead Mottlegill)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also found in pastures but much larger and grows on dung.

lookAlikes.name: Conocybe species (Cone Heads)
lookAlikes.distinction: Some Conocybe species are dangerously toxic and grow in similar grassland habitats.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Galerina (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY POISONOUS — contains amatoxins.

lookAlikes.name: Wavy Cap (Psilocybe cyanescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also grows on wood chips in the Pacific Northwest.

lookAlikes.name: Azurescent Psilocybe (Psilocybe azurescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Larger and more potent, with a caramel-colored cap and broadly convex shape.

lookAlikes.name: Teonanácatl (Psilocybe mexicana)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar small size and also produces sclerotia, but grows at high elevations in Mexican cloud forests.

lookAlikes.name: Liberty Cap (Psilocybe semilanceata)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar size range but has a distinctly pointed, nipple-like cap with a gelatinous pellicle.

lookAlikes.name: Deadly Galerina (Galerina marginata)
lookAlikes.distinction: DEADLY POISONOUS.

lookAlikes.name: Ramaria formosa (Salmon Coral)
lookAlikes.distinction: CAUTION: causes gastrointestinal upset.

lookAlikes.name: Ramaria stricta (Strict-branch Coral)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller, with more upright, parallel branches.

lookAlikes.name: Cauliflower Fungus (Sparassis crispa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Superficially similar in overall shape (large, pale, cauliflower-like), but Sparassis has flat, wavy, ribbon-like lobes rather than branching fingers.

lookAlikes.name: Porcini / King Bolete (Boletus edulis)
lookAlikes.distinction: The prize catch that Satan's Bolete gets confused for.

lookAlikes.name: Le Gal's Bolete (Rubroboletus legaliae)
lookAlikes.distinction: A close relative that's also toxic.

lookAlikes.name: Lurid Bolete (Suillellus luridus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Another blue-bruising bolete with reddish pores — but the cap is dark brown to olive-brown (not pale), and the stem reticulation is darker and coarser.

lookAlikes.name: Peppery Milkcap (Lactarius piperatus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in size, shape, and white coloration, and also has an acrid/peppery taste.

lookAlikes.name: Russula delica
lookAlikes.distinction: Extremely similar white Russula, essentially the European counterpart to R.

lookAlikes.name: Green-spored Parasol (Chlorophyllum molybdites)
lookAlikes.distinction: Both are large whitish mushrooms, but the similarities end there.

lookAlikes.name: Russula virescens (Green-Cracked Russula)
lookAlikes.distinction: Green cap that cracks into a distinctive mosaic pattern of patches.

lookAlikes.name: Russula emetica (The Sickener)
lookAlikes.distinction: Bright red cap (not the variable purple-green-gray of charcoal burner).

lookAlikes.name: Russula ionochlora
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar violet-green coloring but smaller overall.

lookAlikes.name: Beechwood Sickener (Russula nobilis)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar red cap and white gills, but grows exclusively with beech trees rather than conifers.

lookAlikes.name: Russula silvicola (Woodland Sickener)
lookAlikes.distinction: Another red-capped emetic Russula found in mixed and coniferous forests.

lookAlikes.name: Russula integra
lookAlikes.distinction: A brown-red to dark red Russula that is actually edible.

lookAlikes.name: Russula crustosa
lookAlikes.distinction: Also has a cracking cap surface, but the color tends toward yellowish-green, olive, or brownish rather than the clear blue-green of R.

lookAlikes.name: Russula parvovirescens
lookAlikes.distinction: A smaller species with similar green cracking pattern, recently separated from R.

lookAlikes.name: Green-capped Amanita species
lookAlikes.distinction: Some Amanita species can have greenish caps, including A.

lookAlikes.name: Common Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: The key confusion species.

lookAlikes.name: Giant Puffball (Calvatia gigantea)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger (often football-sized or bigger), with smooth white skin and pure white interior.

lookAlikes.name: Leopard Earthball (Scleroderma areolatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller with a thinner skin and finer, more leopard-spot-like scales.

lookAlikes.name: Sparassis spathulata (Eastern Cauliflower Mushroom)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in overall form but grows at the base of hardwoods (oaks, especially) rather than conifers.

lookAlikes.name: Grifola frondosa (Hen of the Woods)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also a large, clustered fungus growing at the base of trees, but Hen of the Woods has distinct, fan-shaped, overlapping caps (each with pores underneath) rather than the wavy, ribbon-like lobes of Sparassis.

lookAlikes.name: Trametes versicolor (Turkey Tail)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very commonly confused.

lookAlikes.name: Stereum gausapatum (Bleeding Oak Crust)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in appearance, but the underside of S.

lookAlikes.name: Stereum complicatum (Crowded Parchment)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar growth habit on dead wood, but forms smaller, more crowded, and more tightly overlapping shelves.

lookAlikes.name: Agaricus species (Field Mushrooms)
lookAlikes.distinction: Some Agaricus species have similar cap shapes and dark spore prints.

lookAlikes.name: Hypholoma sublateritium (Brick Cap)
lookAlikes.distinction: Also reddish-capped and found on wood.

lookAlikes.name: Stropharia aeruginosa (Verdigris Agaric)
lookAlikes.distinction: Same genus, similar shape and ring structure.

lookAlikes.name: Suillus luteus (Slippery Jack)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar slimy cap but has a prominent ring (annulus) on the stem and a purplish to chocolate-brown cap.

lookAlikes.name: Suillus bovinus (Jersey Cow Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Duller, more pinkish-brown cap.

lookAlikes.name: Boletus edulis (King Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more robust with a non-slimy cap, prominent white reticulation on the stem, and white pores when young.

lookAlikes.name: Suillus granulatus (Weeping Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in size, color, and habitat.

lookAlikes.name: Suillus collinitus
lookAlikes.distinction: Has a similar slimy brown cap but is associated with Mediterranean pines (P.

lookAlikes.name: Suillus bovinus (Bovine Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller, with a paler, more olive-toned cap that is less slimy.

lookAlikes.name: False Turkey Tail (Stereum ostrea)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most common mix-up.

lookAlikes.name: Ochre Bracket (Trametes ochracea)
lookAlikes.distinction: A close relative that does have pores underneath, making it harder to distinguish.

lookAlikes.name: Hairy Bracket (Trametes hirsuta)
lookAlikes.distinction: Another close relative with pores underneath.

lookAlikes.name: Cauliflower Mushroom (Sparassis crispa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much larger (often 20–40 cm across), opaque cream to pale tan, and found at the base of living or dead conifer trees — never on small branches.

lookAlikes.name: White Jelly Fungus (Ductifera pululahuana)
lookAlikes.distinction: A rare white jelly fungus that can superficially resemble Tremella fuciformis.

lookAlikes.name: Net-like Tremella (Tremella reticulata)
lookAlikes.distinction: White and gelatinous like T.

lookAlikes.name: Orange Jelly (Dacrymyces chrysospermus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in color and gelatinous texture, but Dacrymyces chrysospermus grows exclusively on dead conifer wood (pine, spruce, fir) — never hardwood.

lookAlikes.name: Yellow Stagshorn (Calocera viscosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar bright yellow color but completely different structure — Calocera viscosa forms firm, upright, antler-shaped or forked branches rather than a blobby, gelatinous mass.

lookAlikes.name: Naematelia aurantia
lookAlikes.distinction: Formerly classified as Tremella aurantia, this species is extremely similar to T.

lookAlikes.name: Sulphur Knight (Tricholoma sulphureum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Superficially similar yellow coloring but has a strong, unpleasant sulfurous or gaslight smell that is impossible to miss.

lookAlikes.name: Tricholoma auratum
lookAlikes.distinction: Very closely related and possibly conspecific (the same species).

lookAlikes.name: Greencracked Brittlegill (Russula virescens)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has a green-cracked cap surface (like dried mud) and brittle white gills.

lookAlikes.name: Western Matsutake (Tricholoma murrillianum)
lookAlikes.distinction: The North American counterpart — often sold as 'matsutake' and nearly identical in appearance and aroma.

lookAlikes.name: Matsutake Look-alike (Tricholoma caligatum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Mediterranean species found under pine and oak in Southern Europe and North Africa.

lookAlikes.name: Smith's Amanita (Amanita smithiana)
lookAlikes.distinction: This is the dangerous one.

lookAlikes.name: Perigord Black Truffle (Tuber melanosporum)
lookAlikes.distinction: More valuable and aromatic.

lookAlikes.name: Common False Truffle (Scleroderma citrinum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Not a true truffle.

lookAlikes.name: Smooth Black Truffle (Tuber macrosporum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Exterior is smoother with flatter warts.

lookAlikes.name: Bianchetto Truffle (Tuber borchii)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much smaller (1-4 cm), with a stronger, almost chemical garlic odor that some find unpleasant.

lookAlikes.name: Oregon White Truffle (Tuber oregonense)
lookAlikes.distinction: Found in the Pacific Northwest of North America, associated with Douglas fir rather than broadleaf trees.

lookAlikes.name: Summer Truffle (Tuber aestivum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar exterior with pyramidal warts, but the interior gleba is much paler, ranging from cream to light brown with white veins, never achieving the dark purplish-black of T.

lookAlikes.name: Chinese Black Truffle (Tuber indicum)
lookAlikes.distinction: Nearly identical in external appearance to T.

lookAlikes.name: Brumale Truffle (Tuber brumale)
lookAlikes.distinction: Smaller than T.

lookAlikes.name: Porcini (Boletus edulis)
lookAlikes.distinction: The mushroom everyone wants.

lookAlikes.name: Pine Bolete (Boletus pinophilus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Darker, reddish-brown cap compared to the Bitter Bolete's paler tan.

lookAlikes.name: Summer Cep (Boletus reticulatus)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very pale, often cracked cap surface (looks 'crazed' like old pottery).

lookAlikes.name: Morchella esculenta (Yellow Morel)
lookAlikes.distinction: True morels have a honeycomb pattern of distinct pits and ridges, not wrinkled brain-like folds.

lookAlikes.name: Morchella elata (Black Morel)
lookAlikes.distinction: Same key differences as yellow morels: cap attached along full length, clean honeycomb pitting, hollow stem.

lookAlikes.name: Gyromitra esculenta (False Morel)
lookAlikes.distinction: Much more irregular and brain-like cap, often reddish brown to dark brown.

lookAlikes.name: Death Cap (Amanita phalloides)
lookAlikes.distinction: The most dangerous confusion.

lookAlikes.name: Destroying Angel (Amanita virosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Another deadly Amanita with a prominent volva.

lookAlikes.name: Volvopluteus gloiocephalus (Stubble Rosegill)
lookAlikes.distinction: A larger species found in agricultural fields and compost in temperate regions.

lookAlikes.name: Xerocomellus porosporus
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar in appearance but has a darker, more uniformly brown cap that cracks less dramatically.

lookAlikes.name: Xerocomus subtomentosus (Suede Bolete)
lookAlikes.distinction: Has a similar olive-brown cap but the surface is velvety (suede-like) rather than cracking to reveal red flesh.

lookAlikes.name: Boletus edulis (Porcini)
lookAlikes.distinction: Beginners sometimes confuse the two, but Boletus edulis is much larger and more robust, with a smooth (not cracking) brown cap, a thick stem with fine white reticulation (net pattern), and a white pore surface that does not bruise blue.

lookAlikes.name: Chrysomphalina chrysophylla
lookAlikes.distinction: Similar tiny golden mushrooms on conifer wood, but typically more yellow than orange, with gills that lack the distinctive cross-veins of Xeromphalina.

lookAlikes.name: Galerina marginata (Deadly Galerina)
lookAlikes.distinction: A critical distinction.

lookAlikes.name: Dead Moll's Fingers (Xylaria longipes)
lookAlikes.distinction: Very similar but noticeably thinner and more elongated, with a longer stalk-like base.

lookAlikes.name: Cordyceps / Ophiocordyceps species
lookAlikes.distinction: Some Cordyceps and Ophiocordyceps species produce dark, club-shaped fruiting bodies that superficially resemble Xylaria.

lookAlikes.name: Yellow Stagshorn (Calocera viscosa)
lookAlikes.distinction: Bright yellow to deep orange, with a rubbery jelly-like texture — nothing like the hard, black, carbonaceous surface of Xylaria.
